title: Leveraging structural disorder to enhance topological phases
authors: Laura Gomez Paz, Peru d'Ornellas, Adolfo G Grushin
abstract: On-site disorder can be leveraged to induce a transition from a trivial to a topological insulator. However it is unclear if structural disorder in the absence of on-site disorder can aid a similar transition and, if so, which kind of structural disorder is more favourable. We numerically show that structural disorder can enhance and sustain a topological phase up to strong disorder in two dimensions provided that one penalises atomic sites from being close to one another. However, we find this effect is absent in three dimensions, where structural disorder appears generically detrimental to the phase. In our calculations we include disorder that can scramble the global spin-reference frame, an overlooked type of disorder expected to exist in strongly disordered solids. This disorder fatally scrambles the information necessary for the spin-Bott and the spin-Chern marker to correctly diagnose a topological phase. By using the spectral localizer, a local marker directly defined using the time-reversal symmetry operator rather than a spin-projection, we show how one can circumvent this limitation, providing a basis-indifferent theory for calculating Z2 invariants. Our work showcases that not all structural disorders are equally beneficial to topology, and highlights guiding principles to enhance and detect topological phases in both solid-state and metamaterial realisations.
summary: This work develops robust theoretical tools, particularly the spectral localizer, to diagnose topological phases in the presence of structural disorder. It reveals that structural disorder's effect is highly dimension-dependent, favoring topological stability in 2D lattices with repulsive interactions but being generally destructive in 3D. This provides key design principles for realizing topological materials in amorphous or disordered solid-state systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates how structural disorder, as opposed to on-site disorder, affects the ability to induce and sustain topological phases in quantum materials.
main_result: Structural disorder can enhance and sustain topological phases in two dimensions if atomic sites are penalized from being too close, but this effect is generally detrimental in three dimensions.
method: The authors employ advanced theoretical diagnostics, notably the spectral localizer, to calculate topological invariants ($\mathbb{Z}_2$ invariants) in real space, circumventing limitations of traditional markers when spin-frame disorder is present.
model_or_system: The study analyzes topological insulators (e.g., BHZ model) on lattices, modeling disorder through structural shifts ($\sigma$) and inter-site repulsion ($ho$). The Hamiltonian incorporates hopping strengths exponentially dependent on atomic separation.
key_observables: Topological invariants ($\mathbb{Z}_2$ invariants), local gap, structure factor, and the spectral localizer index.
important_parameters: Dimensionality (2D vs 3D), structural disorder strength ($\sigma$), inter-site repulsion ($ho$), and the disorder type (structural vs. on-site Anderson disorder $W$).
main_assumptions: The spectral localizer is assumed to provide a basis-independent and robust diagnostic tool, especially when global spin-reference frames are scrambled by disorder.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate phase diagrams comparing topological regimes under Anderson vs. structural disorder in 2D and 3D, and show structure factors comparing systems with and without inter-site repulsion.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the limitations of standard topological markers under disorder, then introducing and validating the spectral localizer, and finally applying this tool to systematically compare the effects of structural disorder in 2D versus 3D systems.
why_interesting: The development of basis-independent topological diagnostics like the spectral localizer is crucial for analyzing complex, disordered quantum systems where symmetries are easily broken or ill-defined.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A comparison between different types of disorder. (a) In a crystal the spin quantization reference frame and coordination is equal across sites. (b) Spin-frame disorder, scrambles the spin-quantization reference frame between sites. (c) In amorphous systems, spin-frame disorder can coexist with structural, bond and on-site disorder. We study the effect of these disorders and point out limitations that they bring to known real-space methods to characterise topology.

FIG. 2. Failure of the spin-Bott index in the presence of spin-frame disorder. (a) As the fraction of sites with spin-frame disorder is in- creased from η = 0 to η = 1 the spin-Bott index from Eq. (14) continuously drops to zero. At η = 1 if fails to diagnose the topologi- cal transition. The spin-Bott index requires a well defined spin gap, which is generically closed by spin-frame disorder. (b) The localizer index from Eq. (8) (with κ = 1) is independent of η and correctly captures the topological phase transition as a function of disorder strength W.

FIG. 3. Two examples of progressively disordering a system starting from a square lattice on the unit square. At each point, bonds are drawn between pairs of sites that are closer than 1.3 times the original lattice spacing r0. (a) With the repulsion parameter, ρ, set to zero, each point is moved to a new position sampled from a Gaussian distribution centred on the original position of the vertex with standard deviation σ. As σ is increased, this tunes smoothly between the square lattice and a set of uniformly distributed vertices. (b) With ρ set to 0.3, the probability of moving a vertex close to any other existing vertices is suppressed. Thus, as the system is progressively disordered by...

Phase diagram of the localizer index for the two-dimensional BHZ model comparing the effect of on-site Anderson and structural disorder, with a small fraction of spin-frame disordered sites η = 0.15, for a system size L = 30. Each point is calculated with A = 1, and averaged over 100 realisations of disorder. The phase diagram is calculated in each case as a function of the parameter M in Eq. (4) and the respective type of disorder. The spectral localizer index given by Eq. (8) is computed for κ = 1 and E, x at the centre of the energy spectrum and the system, respectively. FIG. 5. Phase diagram of the localizer index for the three-dimensional topological insulating model in the presence of Anderson and struc- tural disorder. Each point is calculated with A = 1, and averaged over 100 realizations of disorder, for a system size L = 10. The localizer index, Eq. (10), is computed for κ = 2 and E, x at the bulk of the energy spectrum and the system, respectively. (a) Localizer index for the three-dimensional topological insulator model as a function of Anderson disorder strength W.
